title: Escargots de Bourgogne
subtitle: Snails baked in their shells with garlic-parsley butter
excerpt: Snails filled with a deeply flavoured compound butter of garlic, flat-leaf parsley, and lemon zest, then baked in their shells until the butter is bubbling and fragrant. They take 10 minutes to assemble and 8 minutes to bake. The shells are the serving vessel; the butter is the sauce.
sourceNotes: Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), public domain. Escargots à la bourguignonne preparation including garlic-parsley butter composition and baking method. Note: snails are not in the ingredient lookup and amounts are given in plain prose. Garlic, parsley, butter, and lemon are referenced with scaling tokens as they are available in the lookup.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The garlic-parsley butter can be made up to 3 days ahead and refrigerated, or frozen for up to 1 month. Fill the snail shells a few hours before baking and refrigerate until needed.

glossaryTerms.definition: A classic French compound butter made with softened unsalted butter, finely minced garlic, chopped flat-leaf parsley, salt, and sometimes a little lemon zest. Used specifically for filling escargots shells but also good on grilled fish and steak.

content.text: Escargots de Bourgogne are snails baked in their shells with a

: a compound butter of garlic, flat-leaf parsley, and lemon. The snails themselves taste mild and slightly earthy; the butter is the main event. It bubbles into the shell and pools at the base, where it becomes the most flavourful thing you will eat with bread.

Tinned or jarred snails are the standard choice. They come pre-cooked and are ready to fill. Snail shells are often sold separately (or come packed with the snails). If you cannot find shells, small ramekins with a snail per hollow, covered in butter, work just as well.

The ratio of garlic to butter is the variable that divides people. The Escoffier recipe is generous with the garlic. Start with the amounts below and taste the butter before filling the shells; you can always add more.

You will need 36 snails (from 2 tins of 18, or similar) and 36 snail shells. The snails are not in the ingredient lookup; the amounts below are in plain prose. The butter and aromatics are structured below.

You also need a baking tray or a snail plate (a flat metal dish with 12 round hollows that keep the shells stable during baking). Without a snail plate, press the shells into a bed of coarse salt or crumpled foil to hold them upright.

Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} of softened butter with the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{parsley-flat}} of chopped parsley, and the zest of {{lemon}} lemon until evenly combined. Season well with salt and pepper. Taste the butter: it should be savoury, garlicky, and bright from the parsley. Adjust as needed. Refrigerate for 30 minutes to firm up slightly.

Heat the oven to 220°C. Drain the snails from their tin and pat dry. Push one snail into the opening of each shell. Fill the shell with garlic butter, using about a teaspoon per shell, pressing it in with your finger so the shell is well packed. Arrange the filled shells opening-side up on a baking tray (or in a snail plate). The shells can be refrigerated at this stage for up to 3 hours.

Bake for 7 to 8 minutes until the butter is bubbling vigorously in all the shells. Serve at once on the hot tray with plenty of crusty bread to soak up the butter that pools in the shells and on the tray.

items.symptom: Butter has leaked out of the shells
items.cause: Shells not packed tightly enough, or oven too hot
items.fix: Make sure each shell is filled firmly with butter before baking; bake at 220°C rather than higher, and watch closely

items.cause: Snails baked for too short a time
items.fix: 7 to 8 minutes at 220°C is the minimum; the butter must be fully bubbling and the garlic must have had heat on it to mellow

items.cause: Overcooked; tinned snails are already cooked and only need brief heat
items.fix: 8 minutes is the maximum; they should be just warmed through. More time makes them tough

items.symptom: Shells fall over in the tray
items.cause: No support to hold them upright
items.fix: Press them into a thin bed of coarse salt or scrunch a piece of foil under each shell to create a stable base

content.text: Escargots de Bourgogne is a Burgundian speciality, named for the large Burgundy snail that was gathered from local vineyards. The wine and the snails shared the same terroir. The garlic-parsley butter preparation appears in Escoffier's guide and has remained unchanged since. It is served as a starter in brasseries and bistros throughout France, always with bread and always with white Burgundy or a glass of Chablis alongside.
